title: The 2024 Venice Biennale Asked the Right Questions About Who Gets to Make Art
author: Monica Cosby
body:
[youtube:9LLN1reIJQE]
## Foreigners Everywhere

The 60th Venice Biennale, curated by Adriano Pedrosa under the theme Foreigners Everywhere, delivered on a promise that international art exhibitions often make but rarely keep: it genuinely decentered the Western canon. For the first time in the Biennale's long history, the curatorial vision prioritized artists from the Global South, Indigenous creators, outsider artists, and queer voices in a way that felt substantive rather than tokenistic.

## The Curatorial Vision

Pedrosa's approach was architectural in its logic. The exhibition was organized around the idea that foreignness is a universal condition, that displacement and otherness are experiences shared across cultures and centuries. The result was a show that connected contemporary installation work with historical pieces from artists who had been systematically excluded from the mainstream art world.

The Giardini and Arsenale spaces were transformed into environments that challenged visitors to reconsider their assumptions about artistic lineage. Work by self-taught artists was presented alongside pieces from academically trained creators, and the juxtaposition was revelatory. The hierarchies that typically govern art world presentation were deliberately flattened.

Pedrosa's background as a Brazilian curator at MASP, the Museu de Arte de Sao Paulo, was directly relevant to these choices. MASP has spent years expanding its collection and programming beyond the European historical canon, building one of the most genuinely global collections in the world. He brought that institutional practice to Venice and it showed in every curation decision. The exhibition did not feel like a European institution opening its doors out of obligation. It felt like someone who genuinely knew a different art world had been given control of the space for a season.

## Standout National Pavilions

Several national pavilions delivered exceptional presentations. The range of approaches on display demonstrated that the pavilion format, which skeptics have long criticized as outdated, can still produce genuinely surprising encounters when artists are given freedom and resources.

The Australia Pavilion's presentation by Archie Moore, a Kamilaroi and Bigambul artist from Queensland, was among the most discussed of the Biennale. His installation kith and kin covered the walls and ceiling of the pavilion with an extensive hand-drawn family tree stretching back sixty-five thousand years, placing colonial history within an Indigenous temporal framework that made the violence of that history legible in a new way. The work won the Golden Lion for best national participation, a deserved result that also functioned as an institutional statement about the kind of work the Biennale wanted to be recognizing.

The conversations generated by the pavilions extended well beyond the art press. Questions about national identity, cultural ownership, and the politics of representation animated discussions that reached mainstream media in ways the Biennale had not managed in recent editions.

## What the Outsider Art Inclusion Meant

One of the most significant aspects of Pedrosa's curatorial approach was the integration of outsider art with the same seriousness that the Biennale typically reserves for internationally exhibiting contemporary artists. This was not a ghetto within the show, a separate section for work defined by its makers' marginalization. It was woven throughout.

The effect was to raise a question that serious curators have been avoiding for decades: what have we been missing? If the work of self-taught artists from South America, Africa, and Asia can hold its own on these walls alongside artists represented by major international galleries, what does that tell us about the selection mechanisms that have governed the art world for generations? The answer is not comfortable for the institutions that have profited from those mechanisms.

Pedrosa explicitly connected the history of outsider art's exclusion to the history of colonial exclusion, pointing out that what gets called self-taught is often simply work made outside the Western academic infrastructure. The skill, the intelligence, and the vision were present. The credentials were not. The Biennale treated the credentials as the irrelevant variable they are.

## The Queer Abstraction Thread

A secondary curatorial thread ran through the main exhibition: a genealogy of queer abstraction that traced connections between contemporary LGBTQ+ artists and a largely suppressed history of queer artists working in non-figurative modes across the twentieth century. This thread did important historical recovery work, naming artists and practices that had been present in the historical record but not integrated into the dominant narrative.

The effect was to demonstrate that the decentering of the Western canon and the visibility of queer practice are not separate agendas but overlapping ones. Artists who were excluded by race, by nationality, by sexuality, and by education often fall into multiple categories simultaneously. A curatorial frame that addresses only one axis of exclusion misses most of what has been missed.

## The Golden Lion and What It Named

The Golden Lion for Lifetime Achievement was awarded to Anna Maria Maiolino, an eighty-one-year-old Italian-Brazilian artist who has been making conceptually rigorous work for over six decades with far less recognition than her practice deserves. The choice was perfect and pointed. It placed a specific name and a specific career at the center of the story Pedrosa was telling, a story about all the artists the mainstream art world saw but did not fully recognize.

Maiolino's work moves between drawing, sculpture, performance, and video. Her material choices are often humble, earth and dough and thread, and she uses them to explore cycles of creation and destruction, the body and memory, migration and belonging. These are not marginal concerns. They are the concerns of the present moment, and she has been addressing them rigorously since the 1960s. The recognition came late. It was still right.

## Why This Edition Mattered

The 2024 Venice Biennale mattered because it demonstrated that institutional reform in the art world is possible without sacrificing intellectual rigor. Pedrosa did not water down the exhibition to achieve diversity. He expanded the frame, and in doing so revealed that the canonical narrative of modern and contemporary art has always been incomplete.

This Biennale will be referenced for years as a turning point in how major international exhibitions think about inclusion. It proved that broadening the conversation does not diminish it. It makes it richer, more challenging, and more honest about what art is and who it belongs to.
